does: This paper introduces CoT-Control, a framework for evaluating whether reasoning models can deliberately steer and control their chains of thought when instructed to do so. The authors test advanced reasoning models on their ability to follow explicit directives about how to structure or modify their reasoning processes, finding that these models frequently fail to comply with control instructions. The core contribution demonstrates that even state-of-the-art reasoning systems struggle with fine-grained control over their internal thought processes, with implications for understanding model behavior and alignment.
connects: This work builds on growing research into chain-of-thought reasoning and model interpretability, extending beyond prior work on analyzing emergent reasoning capabilities to now examine whether these capabilities can be reliably directed. It responds to concerns in AI safety literature about model alignment and control, particularly questions about whether advanced reasoning models truly follow operator instructions. The paper connects to earlier work on instruction-following, model steering, and mechanistic interpretability that sought to understand how and why models generate their outputs.
enables: The findings that reasoning models struggle with thought-chain control suggest important directions for AI safety work around model monitorability and auditability. The results point toward developing better mechanisms for enforcing control over reasoning processes, which could improve transparency and reduce risks from unaligned reasoning. This work motivates future research into why control failures occur—whether due to fundamental limitations in how reasoning emerges or correctable training issues—and suggests that monitorability through controllable reasoning could serve as a practical safety layer for advanced reasoning systems.
